What   wi l l   we  cover   t oday?


Unpack the systems development part of IS in terms of drivers,
actors, requirements and approaches


Learn specifically about the conventional SDLC         approaches


Contrast those to recent developments in Agile         systems
development


Understand the importance of being critical and choosing a fit
for purpose approach in systems development

Developing
Information Systems

Background and Drivers

What   i s  syst ems  devel opment ?


Systems development is the entire set of
activities needed to develop/ deploy an
IS/IT solution for a business problem or
opportunity.


In short, it’s how we go about creating     an
information system














                                                                                                                                 5

What   ar e  Syst em  r equi r ement s?


Essentially requirements are a description of exactly what the
systems does     (for a current system), or is expected to do (for a
new system)


Functional requirements describe what a system must do (e.g.
update customer details, generate a report on all transactions
at the end of the day)


Non-functional requirements are characteristic of the system
other than the activities it must perform or support, e.g.
volumes of data it can handle in peak times, security
considerations, user access restrictions and permissions based
on user roles, error detection and recovery, etc


There are also user     and business     re q ui re me nt s


Capturing requirements is a full job in itself (i.e., See our IS unit:
Requirements Analysis and Modelling)
                                                                                                                                           7

What   ar e  t he  dr i ver s  of  I S  devel opment ?



































                                                                                                            8

SDLC
Systems Development Life Cycle

What   i s  Syst em  Devel opment   Li fe  Cycl e  ( SDLC) ?


SDLC  is a phased project model that
encompasses organizational,
personnel, policies, and budgeting
constraints, etc of a business IS/IT
project


The term “project” implies that there
is a beginning and an end to the
cycle


The methods     inherent in SDLC
provide clear, distinct, and defined
phases of work in the elements of
planning, designing, testing,
deploying, and maintaining
information systems.




                                                                                                                                     10

What   ar e  t he  6  cor e  pr ocesses  for   wat er fal l   or
predictive SDLC?














             What’s are the risks with this methodology ?

                                                                                                      11

Are there different variations of SDLC?

























                                                                                12

Agile
The new way

What   i s  Agi l e?


Agile is a way of thinking about and executing a software development project


It’s set of values and principles developed by a community of practitioners in 2001


Frustrated       by   w hat   t hey   saw   as  exce ssi v e   p l anni ng  and   d ocume nt i ng  associ at e d   w i t h  t he   SDLC/
Wa t e r f a l l   me t h o d   a n d   c o n v i n c e d   t h a t   s o f t w a re   d ev e l o p me n t   h a d   l o s t   s i g h t   o f   w h a t   re a l l y
mattered, the customer

What   was  t he  ‘ Agi l e  Mani fest o’ ?


“We are uncovering better ways of developing software by doing it and helping others do it.
Through this work we have come to value:”


Individuals and interactions ov e r        processes and tools


Wo r k i n g   s o f t w a re  ov e rcomprehensive documentation


Customer collaboration ov e r        contract negotiation


Responding to change ov e r        following a plan











                                         Ref. https://www.atlassian.com/agile/manifesto

16

How has the Agi l e concept taken hol d?


The term is now broadly used in all parts of IT (not only systems development):
Project management
            Architecture Design
                         Te     s     t     i     n    g           a     n    d           M    a     i     n    t     e     n    a     n    c     e
                                      Requirements analysis


The tenets of Agile were originally directed toward software development, but since 2001 the
term and tenets have been applied to a broad range of disciplines and activities
Automotive
            Engineering
                         Biomedical research
                                      Management
                                                   Defense
                                                                Government

How does Agi l e work i n practi ce?


The term ‘Agile’ is often used to describe an iterative and incremental process  for ‘doing’
something


‘Chunck’ a problem down to manageable pieces to reduce problem ambiguity and increase
certainty about what the solution looks like


Example: The development of a student admin system is chunked into very small iterative pieces
of work done in that aggregate/ ‘roll-up’ to an enterprise solution


It’s often held up to represent a better way of doing IT projects, but in reality, it can surface the
bad ‘stuff  ’ (conflict, uncertainty…)  a lot quicker


How do you cost a project when you don’t have clarity over the full solution until the very end?

What   i s  Scr um?


Essentially, scrum methodology ‘chunks’ and iterates the development of an application into
short concentrated time frames
           Fundamentally a way of time-boxing           software dev
           People have particular roles
           Ty p e s   o f   ‘ceremonies’ and artifacts


The two terms (Agile and Scrum) are often used often and interchangeably to mean a way of
doing iterative and incremental software work


However, interchangeable use of agile and scrum is wrong
           Agile  –values and principles underlying a particular way of doing software dev
           Scrum   – is methodology for ‘doing’ software dev

How does Scrum work?


Product Backlog –    running ‘to-do’  list
All the dev tasks that need to be completed
for the project to be successful
A list of shippable pieces of work based on
business requirements
Can re-order and re-prioritise     list based on
need
Select tasks from the list to work on
Can also be fixes


Ceremonies

Scrum Principles and Roles

What   ar e  t he  Scr um  r ol es?


Scrum Master
           The “scrum expert ”
           Provide coaching as required on the scrum process
           Assist with the refinement of scrum practices


Product Owner
           Responsible for defining what solution/ success of the ‘chunked’
           piece of development work looks like
           Need to understand how that chunked work will deliver the
           ex p e ct e d   b usi ne ss  b e ne fi t s
           Champion the development work
           Provide problem and solution clarity as required
           Sign-off

What   ar e  t he  Scr um  r ol es?  ( cont . )


Development Team Members
           System/ Solution Architects
           Designers
           Developers
           Te     s     t     e     r     s
           Responsible for designing the solution to the chunked work and developing that
           solution
           Plan and schedule work to be done, problem solving on their feet

What   ar e  t he  Scr um  r ol es?  ( cont . )


Users
           Wo r k   w i t h   d ev e l o p me n t   t e a ms


UAT/ SI roles
           Stakeholders
           Provide guidance to dev teams as required
           There to facilitate the ‘socialization’ of the chunked solution
           Provide a level of assurance and risk management

Let’s Build a Car!
The Agile Way (Example)

1. Define the Requirments



Who are the user and stakeholders?

How do we define the features of the car?
 • Use cases
 • Te   c   h    n    i   c   a   l       s   p    e   c   i   f   i   c   a   t   i   o    n    s
 • Diagrams
What do these features do, how should they perform?

What is the car used for?

Who will use the car and in what capacity: driver, passenger?

What regulations does the car need observe?

……

2. Create the Product Backlog


                           Steering          Must be operational to one person only
                           Systems           Must be operational when car is ‘on’
                                             Must be integrated with ignition system
                                             Must be integrated with drive train
                                             ….
                                             Must turn right
                                             Must turn left
                                             Must turn in forward motion
                                             Must turn in reverse motion
                                             Must have turning circle of <12. 3 meters
                                             …..

4. Do the Spri nts



               First Scrum                                         Day 2

               • Assemble in dedicated space                       • Review previous day  ’s work
               • Hold daily scrum mtg and define                     • What  was  compl eted  what  was
                 what is to be done for that day                       not
               • Define the goal for that day  ’s                  • Re-prioritise day 2 work as

               • Moni tor  progress                                • Do the work
               • Adjust plan as required                           • Moni tor  progress
               • …                                                 • Adjust plan as required

5. Review the Sprints


Sprint Review
This may occur toward the end of the allocated time or progressively through the sprint
Re-assess the product backload
Include fixed in backlog as required
Confirm completed work with stakeholders


Sprint Retrospective
Review feature/ product development process
Review rate of feature/ product development
Identify areas for continuous improvement

Practitioner
Perspective
The good, the bad, the…

What   i s  i t   l i ke  t o  appl y  Agi l e  i n  pr act i ce?


Problematic à     Agile can make projects more complex


Sprint planning has multiple dependencies and fragile critical path
Requirements must be documented and confirmed; Stakeholders must sign-off; Developers must confirm
the work is do-able; Platforms and environments and tools must be available as required; Development
environment, test environment, QA environment, production environment


Can loose sight of the user interaction (how the feature will be used in the real-world)
Who is responsible for providing access to platforms and tools; Who has oversight of the complete end-to-
end enterprise solution; Who documents the solutions as they are developed and who is accountable for
providing assurance that the sprints are delivering what is expected and documented; What happens when
some component essential for the day-to-day operations of the business catches fire and development
team members are unable to participate in the scrum


How do you structure project costs in an agile project

How about a war story?


Company is one of the world’s largest agronomy businesses in the world; Multiple divisions –
corporate, commercial and scientific


Project is a digital transformation initiative that will replace the current fragmented and bespoke
solutions supporting the quote-to-fulfilment process for a key class of products and will radically
improve the way customers interact with the business and its products and how the business
interacts and engages with its customers


Project is building on three SAP Cloud platforms: BTP, Commerce, and Cloud for Customer with
diverse technology stacks and frontend solutions (Fiori and Spartacus).


This development work will roll out in 10 back-to-back sprints, aggregating to three release
packages between Jan-July 2022 with final testing in August, before piloting in  Sept.


Wo r k i n g   i n   S a a S  environments (headless apps –Kubernetes and Kyma         runtime environments)
using Agile

Project Architecture
